Facts.text: Roman Lower Rhine army proclaims its commander, Vitellius, Emperor
The Alamanni cross the frozen Rhine River in large numbers and invade the Roman Empire
John II begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Habsburg Spain and Catholic France sign the secret Treaty of Joinville to end Protestantism in Europe
Spanish forces in Ireland surrender to the English army at Kinsale
Staatsoper Hamburg opens with Theiles "Adam und Eva"
British troops occupy Calcutta, India
Austria ends the use of interrogation by torture
Emperor Joseph II orders Jews of Galicia Austria to adopt family names
Big Bottom Massacre in the Ohio Country begins the Northwest Indian War
Free African American community of Philadelphia petitions US Congress to abolish the slave trade
Institution of Civil Engineers is founded in Britain
Lord Byroncompletes the long narrative poem "Childe Harold's Pilgrimage" (4th canto)
1st Curling club in US (Orchard Lake Curling Club) opens
Re-establishment of British rule over the Falkland Islands
First US wire suspension bridge for general traffic opens in Pennsylvania
Colonel Charles Stone is put in charge of organizing DC militia
South Carolina seizes inactive Ft Johnson in Charleston Harbor
Battle of Murfreesboro (Stone's River) ends
Welterweight Con Orem & heavyweight Hugh O'Neill brawl for 185 rounds before darkness ends legendary bare knuckle boxing match in Virginia City, Montana
King Amadeus I of Spain is inaugurated at 25
British battleship HMS Thunderer gun turret explodes during gunnery practice in Gulf of Izmit, near Turkey; 11 sailors killed and 35 injured
Dr Benjamin Mays, named president of Atlanta Board of Education
Because of anti-monopoly laws, Standard Oil is organized as a trust
Battle of Boschberg takes place in South Africa as part of the Mapoch War
General Wolseley receives last distress signal of General Gordon in Khartoum
Alice Sanger becomes the first female White House staffer
Record 19.2-foot alligator is shot in Louisiana by American businessman Edward Avery McIlhenny
First US commemoratives and first US stamp to picture a woman issued (Queen Isabella, patron of Columbus)
World's Columbian Exposition opens in Chicago [or 0501]
Battle of Doornkop: Boers defeat Dr. Jameson's troops in South Africa
Émile Berliner begins manufacturing 7-inch single-sided records in Montreal

Conference of 23 industrial trade unionists in Chicago, Illinois, issues the "Industrial Union Manifesto," calling for a convention to be held in Chicago in June and laying the groundwork for the formation of the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW)
Elara, a satellite of Jupiter, discovered by Charles Dillon Perrine in San Jose, California
Japanese troops capture Port Arthur from Russia, a blow to national morale and causing further discontent within Russia
Canadian branch of the Royal Mint opens in Ottawa
First official Dutch Eleven Cities Skate (Minne Hoekstra in 13:50)
1st junior high schools in US opens (Berkeley California)
Australia releases its first national stamp - a kangaroo on a map of Australia
Philips installs research department in Eindhoven
Royal Bank of Canada takes over Quebec Bank
Montreal Arena in Westmount, Quebec, the home rink of 4-time Stanley Cup winners the Montreal Wanderers burns down leading to the club disbanding
Responding to global fear of communism caused by the Russian Revolution, US Attorney General Palmer authorizes raids across the country on unionists and socialists
First religious service radio broadcast in the US over KDKA in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
The M. H. de Young Memorial Museum of fine arts opens in Golden Gate Park, San Francisco, California
Ku Klux Klan surprise attack on black residential area Rosewood Florida, 8 killed (compensation awarded in 1995)
Book publisher Simon & Schuster is founded in New York by Richard L. Simon and M. Lincoln Schuster
Gorno-Badakhshan Autonomous Region established (now in Tadzhik SSR)
Melody Maker, British popular music magazine, publishes 1st issue
US and Canada agree to preserve the Niagara Falls
Young gang shoots dead six police officers in Springfield, Missouri
Ijmuider fishing strike begins (till July 11th)
US troops leave Nicaragua
First state liquor stores open in Pennsylvania
First electron tube to enable night vision is described in St Louis, Missouri
World War II: German bombing severely damages the Llandaff Cathedral in Cardiff, Wales
World War II: US government announces its Liberty ship program to build freighters in support of the war effort
German troops in Bardia, Libya surrender
Japanese troops occupy Manila, Philippines
World War II: The 28 nations at war with the Axis powers pledge to make no separate peace deals
University of Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball team begins 129 home game winning streak that only ends in 1955; incorporates NCAA titles in 1948, 1949 and 1951
Allied air raid on Nuremberg
Radio Orange ends cooperation at Liese-Aktion
Mahatma Gandhibegins a march for peace in East Bengal
WNDT (now WNET) TV channel 13 in New York-Newark, NY (PBS) begins
KDKA TV channel 2 in Pittsburgh, PA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Luis Muñoz Marín becomes the first democratically elected Governor of Puerto Rico
Philip Barry's play "Second Threshold," premieres in NYC
NBA Baltimore Bullets begin a record 32 game road losing streak (12 games in 1952-53; 20 games in 1953-54) with a 73-66 defeat at the Indianapolis Olympians
Panamanian President José Antonio Remón is assassinated
The first "The Bob Cummings Show" premieres on NBC (later moves to CBS)
Poujadists/Communists win French parliamentary elections
Dmitri Shostakovich' 2nd Piano Concerto has US premiere, with Leonard Bernstein as soloist and conductor of the New York Philharmonic at Carnegie hall in NYCs in NY
First redshank, an Old World shorebird, reported in North America (Halifax)
John Reynolds sets age of solar system at 4,950,000,000 years
Hawaii's then all-time low temperature, 14°F, is recorded atop Haleakalā
Nighttime version of "Password" with Allen Ludden premieres on CBS
Ayub Khan elected President of Pakistan
Obverse design of all Canadian coins is changed to depict present-day
First Jewish child born in Spain since 1492 expulsion
KBHK TV channel 44 in San Francisco, California (IND) begins broadcasting
Luis Ferré becomes the first statehooder Governor of Puerto Rico
Operation Barrier Reef begins in the Mekong Delta, Vietnam
Robert B. Nemiroff's stage drama "To Be Young, Gifted & Black" adaptated from the writings of his late wife of Lorraine Hansberry, starring Ruby Dee, premieres in NYC
The Beatles begin rehearsals, while being filmed, for the Let It Be project at Twickenham Film Studios, London
Dutch premiere of Galt MacDermot, Gerome Ragni and James Rado's hippie musical "Hair" in Amsterdam
US population is 293,200,000; African American population: 22,600,000 (11.1%)

Spectator crush at Ibrox Park in Glasgow, Scotland as Rangers supporters leave the ground with home team 0-1 behind to Old Firm rivals Celtic; 66 deaths and more than 200 injuries; Rangers score late for 1-1
An anti-internment rally is held in Belfast, North Ireland
Mariner 9 begins mapping Mars
Japanese boxer Masao Ohba retains his WBA flyweight title with a 12th-round knockout of Chartchai Chionoi of Thailand in Tokyo; Ohba dies three weeks later in a car accident
Worst fire in Argentine history destroys 1.2 million acres
US Department of Interior designates grizzly bear a threatened species
Rhino Records releases their 1st album "Wildmania", by Larry "WIld Man" Fischer
British Steel workers go on a national strike
New York Islanders beat Philadelphia Flyers, 3-1 at Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum to start 23 NHL game undefeated home streak (21-0-2), 14 straight wins
The "Epic in Miami," in 85°F heat, the San Diego Chargers defeat the Miami Dolphins 41-38 in overtime in the AFC Divisional Playoff Game, a game that sets numerous playoff scoring records
Gary Trudeau takes a 20-month break from writing "Doonesbury"

In a 35-27 win over the Houston Oilers, Cincinnati Bengals quarterback Ken Anderson completes 20 consecutive passes and finishes the season winning his second consecutive passing title with an NFL record 70.55% completion percentage
Several African National Congress (ANC) members detained in Swaziland decide to leave the country voluntarily for Mozambique.
16-year-old future South African Test cricket batsman Darryl Cullinan scores his 1st first-class century (106 no) for Border against Natal B in East London
Riot in Tunis kills over 100
Wilson Goode, sworn-in as Philadelphia's 1st black mayor
Leg-spin bowler Bob "Dutchy" Holland takes 4/90 for match figures of 10/144 as Australia beats the West Indies by innings in 5th Test in Sydney; Windies take series, 3-1
Nevada Las Vegas basketball gives coach Jerry Tarkanian his 600th career victory with a 142-140 win over Utah State in triple overtime; total of 282 points breaks NCAA men's record of 275 set in 1976
Indiana Pacers beat the Los Angeles Clippers, 116-106 to give coach Jack Ramsay his 800th NBA victory; at the time Ramsey is one of only 2 coaches (with Red Auerbach) to reach milestone
Ashland Oil storage tank spills 3.8 million gallons in Pennsylvania
Right-wing guerrillas ambush a train near Mozambique's western border, killing at least 22 people and injuring 71.
"Superwoman" single released by Karyn White (Billboard Song of the Year 1989)
UCLA beats Arkansas Razorbacks, 17-3 to win the Cotton Bowl Classic in Dallas; record 7th consecutive football bowl game victory for the Bruins
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 2,800 (2,810.15)
University of Colorado, Boulder, coached by Bill McCartney wins its first Associated Press National Championship of College Football poll
Opposing factions in the Bosnian conflict hold meetings with the aim of ending the nine month conflict
Battles between the army and rebellious Indigenous people in southern Mexico kill 57
Bus crashes in Luzon Philippines, 29 killed
Most distant galaxy yet discovered found by scientists using Keck telescope in Hawaii (est 15 billion light years away)
The US deploys troops in Northern Bosnia with the intention of maintaining order and peace between Bosnian Serbs and Muslims
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Columbus, Ohio on WBZX 99.7 FM

A brutal snowstorm smashes into the Midwestern United States, causing 14 inches (359 mm) of snow in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, and 19 inches (487 mm) in Chicago, where temperatures plunge to -13°F (-25°C); 68 deaths are reported
19,600 fans watch the Miami Heat beat the Orlando Magic, 111-103 in the first NBA game at the Heat's new American Airlines Arena in Miami
Sila Calderón becomes the first female Governor of Puerto Rico
Eduardo Duhalde is appointed interim President of Argentina by the Legislative Assembly
Stardust successfully flies past Comet Wild 2, collecting samples that it will return to Earth two years later
Sago Mine Disaster: 12 miners are killed in a coal mine explosion in West Virginia
Englishman Adrian Lewis retains his PDC World Darts Championship with a 7-3 win over Andy Hamilton at the Alexandra Palace, London
30 people are killed after a bus plunges off a cliff and falls 400 ft in Malshej Ghat, India
Saudi Arabia executes 47 alleged terrorists, including Shia cleric Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr
US House Republicans vote to gut the independent Office of Congressional Ethics, a public uproar forces them to back down the next day
Bus crash in Pasamayo, Peru, kills 51 on notorious "Devil's Curve" road
Storm Eleanor forms near Ireland then sweeps across the UK and Western Europe in next few days
US senator Al Franken resigns
WHO reveals it will classify gaming addiction as mental health condition in its next Classification of Diseases
Two women become the first to ever enter India's Sabarimala shrine in Kerala State, after law change, prompting protests

United States international Christian Pulisic becomes the most expensive American soccer player when he moves from Borussia Dortmund to Chelsea for £57.6M ($73M); remains at Dortmund on loan until the end of the season
Israel becomes one of the first countries to offer a fourth vaccine dose against COVID-19 amid an Omicron surge
Sudan’s Prime Minister Abdalla Hamdok resigns in a televised address amid political deadlock after a rejection of his deal with the military by pro-democracy forces
Buffalo Bills player Damar Hamlin collapses in cardiac arrest and is revived by CPR on the field in televised NFL game against the Bengals in Cincinnati
Two helicopters collide in midair on the Gold Coast, Australia, killing four and injuring eight
Claudine Gay, Harvard University's first Black president, resigns following testimony to Congress on anti-semitism and amid plagiarism allegations

Japan Airlines Flight 516 crashes into Coast Guard plane and burns during landing at Haneda airport in Tokyo; all 379 passengers and crew on board the JAL Airbus evacuate safely, 5 of the 6 Coast Guard members are killed
TsarIvan the Terrible's march to Novgorod begins
George Washington's army hoists the Grand Union Flag at Prospect Hill, Charlestown
Timothy Pickering of Massachusetts becomes the first US Senator censured by the body for revealing confidential documents of PresidentThomas Jefferson
Lord Byroncompletes "The Corsair",  a long tale in verse
First photo of the Moon, taken by French photographerLouis Daguerre
Richard Wagner's opera "The Flying Dutchman" premieres in Dresden, Germany
Camille Saint-Saëns' 3rd Violin Concerto in B, premieres in Paris, France, with Spanish virtuoso Pablo de Sarasate as soloist and dedicatee
The SS Commodore, the shipStephen Craneis aboard, founders off the Florida coast, an experience he recounts in his short story "The Open Boat"
US Secretary of StateJohn Hayannounces the Open Door Policy to promote trade with China
US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltshuts down post office in Indianola, Mississippi, for refusing to accept its appointed postmistress because she was black
Willis Carrierreceives a US patent for the world's first air conditioner
Brookyln Superbas Baseball Club PresidentCharles Ebbetsannounces purchase of 4.5 acres of land to build a new concrete-and-steel stadium to seat 23,000; Ebbets Field opens in 1913
Mahatma Gandhileaves the Tolstoy Farm in Transvaal, South Africa
After repeated clashes over pay with the Brooklyn Robins owner Charlie Ebbets, star right fielder and future Baseball Hall of Fame managerCasey Stengelis traded to the Pittsburgh Pirates
Bruno Hauptmann trial begins for kidnap-murder ofCharles Lindbergh's baby
In the first of 4 meetings between the fighters, Gene Fullmer wins the world middleweight boxing title with a 15-round unanimous decision overSugar Ray Robinsonat New York’s Madison Square Garden
Roger Sessions' 4th Symphony, in honor of the Minnesota Centennial, premieres by the Minnesota Orchestra conducted by Antal Doráti
SenatorJohn F. Kennedy, announces his candidacy for the US Presidency
Failed assassination attempt on PresidentKwame Nkrumahof Ghana
Martin Luther King Jr.begins a drive to register black voters
New York Jets sign future Pro Football Hall of Fame quarterbackJoe Namathto a $427,000 contract over three years (pro football record at the time)
Cecil Day-Lewis is appointed British Poet Laureate byQueen Elizabeth II
Christiaan Barnardperforms the world's second heart transplant on Philip Blaiberg
AustralianRupert Murdochgains control of the 'News of the World'

Once influential daily sports newspaper, 'Soviet Sport' declares Czech super-star distance runner Emil Zatopek a "public enemy" after he supports democratic wing of the Communist Party at start of the Prague Spring
55 MPH speed limit is imposed byRichard Nixon
MLB CommissionerBowie Kuhnsuspends Atlanta Braves ownerTed Turnerfor one year due to tampering charges in Gary Matthews free-agency signing
British punk rockerSid Vicious' trial for murder of girlfriend Nancy Spungen begins in New York City
Major-GeneralMuhammadu Buhariis declared Head of State in Nigeria, following a military coup
Egyptian PresidentHosni Mubarakreappoints Coptic pope Shenuda III

NHL New York Islanders right wingMike Bossyscores his 499th and 500th career goals in the final 2:22 to lift the New York to a 7-5 victory over the Boston Bruins; 11th player in NHL history to score 500 goals
Troops of Chadian PresidentHissène Habréconquer the Fada oasis
Prime MinisterBrian MulroneyandRonald Reagansign Canada-US Free Trade Agreement
Australian cricket spin bowling greatShane Warne(145 Tests, 708 wickets; 194 ODIs) makes Test debut v India in 3rd Test at Sydney Cricket Ground
Autopsy ofChris Farleyshows he overdosed of opiates and cocaine
Raúl Castrogives a speech commemorating the 55th anniversary of the Cuban revolution and warns of "neo-liberal and neo-colonial thinking" entering the country
The Economist listsDaniel Kahnemanas the 7th most influential economist in the world
Michael van Gerwenof the Netherlands wins his 2nd PDC World Darts Championship beating defending champion Gary Anderson, 7-3 at the Alexandra Palace, London
Apple CEOTim Cookblames below expectation Chinese iPhone sales for downturn in company's outlook, rocking international stock markets
US PresidentDonald Trumpsays to Georgia's secretary of state Brad Raffensperger "I just want to find 11,780 votes, which is one more than we have,” in recording released by the Washington Post
US epidemiologist Dr.Anthony Faucisays focus should be on hospitalizations not case numbers amid huge surge in Omicron COVID-19 cases. Cases up 202%, hospitalizations up 30% in US.
Muhammad XII, the last Emir of Granada, surrenders his city toFerdinand II of Aragonand Isabella I of Castile, ending both the Reconquista and centuries of Muslim rule on the Iberian Peninsula
Georgia is the fourth state to ratify the US Constitution
USSR launches the Luna 1 spacecraft (Mechta), the 1st to leave Earth's gravity, reach the vicinity of the Moon and to be placed in heliocentric orbit
Yōzei, 57th Emperor of Japan (876-84), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 949)
Mehmed IV, Turkish Sultan of the Ottoman Empire (1648-87), born in Topkapı Palace, Constantinople (d. 1693)
Nathaniel Bacon, British-born American colonist (leader of 1676 Bacon's Rebellion, Virginia), born in Suffolk, England (d. 1676)
Osman III, sultan of Turkey (1754-57), born in Edirne Palace, Edirne (d. 1757)
Marie Dumesnil, French actress (Iphigénie en Aulide), born in Paris, France (d. 1803)
Jacques-Alexandre Laffon de Ladebat, French shipbuilder and merchant of the port of Bordeaux, born in Bayonne, France (d. 1797)
Louis di Belgioioso, Austrian count, diplomat and field marshal under the Habsburgs, born in Belgioioso (d. 1801)
František "Franz" Xaver Brixi, Czech classical composer and kapellmeister, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1771)
Philip Freneau, American poet of the American Revolution (The American Village), born in New York City (d. 1832)
Christian Daniel Rauch, German sculptor (Frederick II of Prussia equestrian statue), born in Arolsen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1857)
Jan Gelinde van Blom, Dutch Frisian notary, poet, and folksong writer, born in Harlingen, Batavian Republic (d. 1871)
Gugliemo Libri [della Somaia], Italian mathematician and book collector (stole 30,000 books), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1869)
Tomasz Napoleon Nidecki, Polish composer, born in Studzianka, West Galicia (d. 1852)
Friedrich Wilhelm Jahns, German composer, voice teacher and musicologist, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1888)
Rudolf Clausius, German physicist (one of the founders of the study of thermodynamics), born in Köslin Prussia (d. 1888)
Pyotr Semyonov-Tyan-Shansky, Russian explorer, geographer, and statistician (manager of the Russian Geographical Society), born in Russia (d. 1914)
Henry Kingsley, English Australian writer, born in Northamptonshire (d. 1876)
Justin Winsor, American librarian (Boston Public Library, 1866-78; Harvard, 1878-97), founder of American Library Association, and writer, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1897)
Frederick A. Johnson, American politician, banker, and US Representative from New York, born in Fort Edward, New York (d. 1893)
Charles Russell Lowell Jr, American railroad executive and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1864)
Mendele Moykher Sforim [Sholem Abramovich], Lithuanian-Jewish writer (Dos Vinshfingeril (The Wishing Ring)), born in Kapyl, Minsk Governorate. Russian Empire (d. 1917)
Queen Emma of Hawaii, Consort of King Kamehameha IV (1856-63), born in Honolulu, Kingdom of Hawaii (d. 1885)
Mily Balakirev, Russian pianist and composer (Islamej, Tamara), born in Nizhny Novgorod, Russia (d. 1910)
Amy Gaston Bonet-Maury, French reformed theologist, born in Paris (d. 1919)
Sandor Erkel, Hungarian composer and son of Ferenc Erkel, born in Pest, Hungary (d. 1900)
Frank Hadow, British tennis player (Wimbledon 1878; first exponent of the lob), born in London, England (d. 1946)
Frederick Burr Opper, American pioneering cartoonist (Happy Hooligan, Alphonse & Gaston), born in Madison, Ohio (d. 1937)
Martha Carey Thomas, American educator, suffragist, linguist and second President of Bryn Mawr College, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1935)
William Corless Mills, American museum curator (excavated Ohio Indian mounds), born in Pyrmont, Ohio. (d. 1928)
Helen Herron Taft, American First lady (1909-13) as wife of President William Howard Taft, planted Washington's cherry trees, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1943)
Lucia Zarate, Mexican entertainer (lightest known adult human - 2.1 kg at 17), born in Veracruz, Mexico (d. 1890)
Gilbert Murray, Australian professor and scholar of Ancient Greece, born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1957)
Arthur Gore, British tennis player (Wimbledon 1901, 08-09), born at Lydhurst, England (d. 1928)
Ernst Barlach, German writer and artist, born in Wedel (d. 1938)
Tex Rickard, American boxing promoter and entrepreneur (founder NHL New York Rangers; builder third incarnation of Madison Square Garden, NYC), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1929)
Anton Pannekoek, Dutch astronomer/marxist theorist (Communist Tactics), born in Vaassen, Netherlands (d. 1960)
Moisei Uritsky, Russian Bolshevik revolutionary (leader of Petrogradse Cheka), born in Cherkasy, Kiev Governorate (d. 1918)
Therese of Lisieux, French Roman-Catholic nun, made a saint in 1925, born in Lisieux, France (d. 1897)[1]
Slava Raskaj, Croatian painter known for her watercolors (Water Lilies, 1898), born in Ozalj, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1906)
Jan [Johannes Lodewijk] Walch, Dutch literary figure (Grimaces, New Handbook of Literary History), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1946)[1]
Pieter Tesch, Dutch geologist, known for making a new geological map of the Netherlands, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1961)
Sydney Baynes, British conductor, arranger, bandleader and composer (Destiny Waltz; Endure to Conquer), born in Sudbury, England (d. 1938)
Louis Charles Breguet, French aviation pioneer (founded the Compagnie des messageries aérienne which became Air France), born in Paris (d. 1955)
Jacques Chardonne [Boutelleau], French writer (l'Epithalame), born in Barbezieux-Saint-Hilaire, France (d. 1968)

José Méndez, Cuban Baseball HOF pitcher (NgL World Series 1924; 
3 × NgL NL pennants; Kansas City Monarchs), born in Cárdenas, Cuba (d. 1928)
Apsley Cherry-Garrard, British Antarctic explorer and survivor of the Terra Nova Expedition (The Worst Journey in the World), born in Bedford, England (d. 1959)
Florence Lawrence [Bridgwood], Canadian stage and silent screen actress (Confidence; Lady Helen's Escapade), born in Hamilton, Ontario (d. 1938)
Moyshe Leyb Halpern, Galican-American Yiddish poet (In New-York), born in Zlotshev, Galicia (d. 1932)
Walter Baldwin, American actor (The Best Years of Our Lives, The Long, Long Trailer), born in Lima, Ohio (d. 1977)
Lura Anson, American silent film actress, born in Nebraska (d. 1968)
Lillian Leitzel [Leopoldina Alitza Pelikan], German acrobat and strongwoman, born in Breslau, Germany (d. 1931)
Robert Nathan, American poet and novelist (Portrait of Jennie), born in New York City (d. 1985)
Folke Bernadotte, Swedish diplomat (Red Cross; UN mediator for Palestine), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1948)
Dziga Vertov [Denis Kaufman], Russian director and film theorist (Man with a Movie Camera, Sjagai, Soviet!), born in Białystok, Russian Empire (d. 1954)
Ernst-Lothar von Knorr, German composer, and music educator, born in Eitorf, Germany (d. 1973)
Lawrence Wackett, Australian aircraft engineer and general manager of the Commonwealth Aircraft Corporation (1936-60), born in Townsville, Australia (d. 1982)
Jimmy Nervo [James Holloway], British comedian (Nervo and Knox), and actor (Frozen Limits), born in Dalston, England (d. 1975)
Paul-Henri Spaak, Belgian politician (Prime Minister of Belgium, Secretary-General of NATO 1957-61), born in Schaerbeek, Belgium, (d. 1972)
William Haines, American actor (Fast Life, Little Annie Rooney), born in Staunton, Virginia (d. 1973)
Rex O'Malley, British actor (Camille; Zara; Midnight, Thief), born in London, England (d. 1976)
Robert "Bob" Marshall, American forester, wilderness activist and co-founder of the Wilderness Society, born in New York City (d. 1939)
Torsten Ralf, Swedish operatic tenor (Daphne - "Apollo"), born in Malmö, Sweden (d. 1954)

Dan Keating, Irish former president of the Republican Sinn Féin, Ireland's oldest man and last surviving veteran of the Irish War of Independence at the time of his death, born in Castlemaine, Ireland (d. 2007)
Anton van Duinkerken [Willem Jan Marie Anton Asselbergs], Dutch poet and academic, born in Bergen op Zoom, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Kane Tanaka, oldest Japanese supercentenarian and 2nd oldest person in the world (119 years, 107 days), born in Wajiro Village, Japan (d. 2022)
James Melton, American pop (1920-30's) and operatic tenor (Metropolitan Opera, 1942-50), born in Moultrie, Georgia (d. 1961)
Walter Heitler, German physicist, quantum field theorist, philosopher, and writer (Man and Science), born in Karlsruhe, German Empire (d. 1981)
Lev Schnirelmann, Soviet-Russian mathematician (number theory, topology and differential geometry), born in Gomel, Belarus, Russian Empire (d. 1938)
Michael Tippett, British conductor and composer (A Child of Our Time), born in London, England (d. 1998)
Edward Albert Radice, British economist, born in London, England (d. 1996)
Salvador Ley, Guatemalan pianist, composer, and educator (National Conservatory; Westchester Conservatory), born in Guatemala City, Guatemala (d. 1985)
Ben Grauer, American newscaster (Big Story), born in New York City (d. 1977)
Jānis Ķepītis, Latvian pianist, conductor, and composer, born in Beverīna, Latvia (d. 1989)
René Étiemble, French scholar, novelist and translator (work on Rimbaud), born in Mayenne, France (d. 2002)
Srirangam Srinivasarao [Sri Sri], Telugu Poet, born in Visakhapatnam, India (d. 1983)
Ulrich Becher, German writer and playwright, born in Berlin (d. 1990)
André Amellér, French double bassist (Paris Opéra, 1937-53), conductor, composer, and educator (École Nationale de Musique - Dijon, 1953-81), born in Arnaville, France (d. 1990)
Barbara Pentland, Canadian concert pianist and composer, born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2000)
Anna Lee [Joan Boniface Winnifrith], British actress (General Hospital, How Green Was My Valley), born in Ightham, Kent, England (d. 2004)
Ernest Sidey, British air marshal (General of RAF Medical Services, Honorary Surgeon to the Queen), born in Alyth, Scotland (d. 2002)
Gardner Read, American composer (Quiet Music for Strings; Dance of the Locomotives), and pedagogue (Boston University, 1948-78), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2005)
Juanita Jackson Mitchell, American lawyer (1st African American to practice law in Maryland, NAACP), born in Hot Springs, Arkansas (d. 1992)
John Hope Franklin, American historian (From Slavery to Freedom, 1995 Presidential Medal of Freedom), born in Rentiesville, Oklahoma (d. 2009)
Nick Fatool, American jazz and session drummer, born in Millbury, Massachusetts (d. 2000)
Vera Zorina [Eva Hartwig], German-Norwegian ballet dancer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2003)
Joan Merrill, American pop vocalist ("There Will Never Be Another You"), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1992)
Willi Graf, German anti-Nazi activist and member of the White Rose resistance group, born in Kuchenheim, Germany (d. 1943)
Andrew Cavendish, Duke of Devonshire, English aristocrat (Chatsworth), politician, married Deborah Mitford, born in London, England (d. 2004)
Peter Harrison Swan, English-Australian WWII bomber pilot and stockbroker, born in London, England (d. 1995)
Glen Harmon, Canadian ice hockey defenceman (Stanley Cup 1944, 46: NHL All Star 1949, 50; Montreal Canadiens), born in Holland, Manitoba (d. 2007)
Jason Evers, American actor (Wrangler, Channing), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Nico Schuyt, Dutch composer, born in Alkmaar, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Andrzej Nikodemovich, Ukrainian-Polish organist, pianist, composer, and teacher, born in Lviv, Ukraine (d. 2017)
Larry Harmon, American entertainer and TV producer (Bozo the Clown), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 2008)
William J. Crowe, Kentucky, American admiral and ambassador, Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff (1985-89), born in La Grange, Kentucky (d. 2007)
Gino Marchetti, Pro Football Hall of Fame defensive end (Pro Bowl x 11), born in Smithers, West Virginia (d. 2019)
Howard Caine, American actor (Brushfire), born in Nashville Tennessee (d. 1993)
David Herbert, English publisher (The Herbert Press), born in London (d. 1996)[1]
Yuri Grigorovich, Soviet Russian dancer, artistic director and choreographer, born in Leningrad, Russia (d. 2025)[1]
Alberto Zedda, Italian opera conductor and musicologist, born in Milan, Italy (d. 2017)
Avie Bennett, Canadian real estate developer, publishing magnate, and philanthropist, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2017)
Daisaku Ikeda, Japanese Buddhist leader (Soka Gakkai), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 2023)[1]
Dan Rostenkowski, American politician (Rep-D-Il, 1959-94), convicted of mail fraud, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2010)
Gerhard Amanshauser, Austrian writer (Schloß mit späten Gästen), born in Salzburg, Austria (d. 2006)
Harry Hyams, British real estate developer (Centre Point -  London), born in Hendon, Middlesex, England (d. 2015)
Oskar Rabin, Russian painter and dissident during Soviet era, born in Moscow, Soviet Union (d. 2018)
Tiberiu Olah, Romanian-Hungarian composer, teacher, and musicologist, born in Batar, Romania (d. 2002)
Vaughn Beals, American CEO (Harley Davidson, 1981-96), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 2018)
